}
36. Read @ -
Input
{
  "file_path": "/workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java",
  "limit": 210,
  "offset": 175
}
Output
{
  "file": {
    "content": "     * timestamp to save space. For CREATE_TIME timestamp type, this is typically the timestamp of the first\n     * record. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, this is the timestamp assigned by the broker. If the batch becomes empty after\n     * compaction, this field is set to {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} (-1).\n     *\n     * <p><b>Performance:</b> This method is O(1) and reads directly from the buffer.\n     *\n     * @return The base timestamp in milliseconds since Unix epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} if\n     *         the batch is empty or the timestamp is undefined.\n     * @see #maxTimestamp() for the maximum timestamp in the batch\n     * @see TimestampType#CREATE_TIME\n     */\n    public long baseTimestamp() {\n        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public long maxTimestamp() {\n        return buffer.getLong(MAX_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public TimestampType timestampType() {\n        return (attributes() & TIMESTAMP_TYPE_MASK) == 0 ? TimestampType.CREATE_TIME : TimestampType.LOG_APPEND_TIME;\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public long baseOffset() {\n        return buffer.getLong(BASE_OFFSET_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public long lastOffset() {\n        return baseOffset() + lastOffsetDelta();\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public long producerId() {\n        return buffer.getLong(PRODUCER_ID_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public short producerEpoch() {\n        return buffer.getShort(PRODUCER_EPOCH_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int baseSequence() {\n        return buffer.getInt(BASE_SEQUENCE_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    private int lastOffsetDelta() {\n        return buffer.getInt(LAST_OFFSET_DELTA_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int lastSequence() {\n        int baseSequence = baseSequence();\n        if (baseSequence == RecordBatch.NO_SEQUENCE)\n            return RecordBatch.NO_SEQUENCE;\n        return incrementSequence(baseSequence, lastOffsetDelta());\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public CompressionType compressionType() {\n        return CompressionType.forId(attributes() & COMPRESSION_CODEC_MASK);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int sizeInBytes() {\n        return LOG_OVERHEAD + buffer.getInt(LENGTH_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    private int count() {\n        return buffer.getInt(RECORDS_COUNT_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public Integer countOrNull() {\n        return count();\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void writeTo(ByteBuffer buffer) {\n        buffer.put(this.buffer.duplicate());\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public void writeTo(ByteBufferOutputStream outputStream) {\n        outputStream.write(this.buffer.duplicate());\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public boolean isTransactional() {\n        return (attributes() & TRANSACTIONAL_FLAG_MASK) > 0;\n    }\n\n    private boolean hasDeleteHorizonMs() {\n        return (attributes() & DELETE_HORIZON_FLAG_MASK) > 0;\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public OptionalLong deleteHorizonMs() {\n        if (hasDeleteHorizonMs())\n            return OptionalLong.of(buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET));\n        else\n            return OptionalLong.empty();\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public boolean isControlBatch() {\n        return (attributes() & CONTROL_FLAG_MASK) > 0;\n    }\n\n    @Override\n    public int partitionLeaderEpoch() {\n        return buffer.getInt(PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH_OFFSET);\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Creates an input stream for reading the records in this batch, handling decompression if necessary.\n     *\n     * <p>This method handles the decompression setup for compressed batches. For uncompressed batches, it simply\n     * wraps the buffer. For compressed batches, it returns a decompression stream.\n     *\n     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> The returned stream does not decompress data until it is actually read.\n     * For large compressed batches, this defers memory allocation and CPU cost until records are accessed.\n     *\n     * @param bufferSupplier Supplies buffers for decompression. For optimal performance with reusable buffers,\n     *                       use {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier} or similar.\n     * @return An InputStream positioned at the start of the records section, with decompression applied if needed.\n     * @throws KafkaException if the compression codec is not available or decompression setup fails\n     */\n    public InputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {\n        final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate();\n        buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET);\n        return Compression.of(compressionType()).build().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier);\n    }\n\n    private CloseableIterator<Record> compressedIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier, boolean skipKeyValue) {\n        final InputStream inputStream = recordInputStream(bufferSupplier);\n\n        if (skipKeyValue) {\n            return new StreamRecordIterator(inputStream) {\n                @Override\n                protected Record doReadRecord(long baseOffset, long baseTimestamp, int baseSequence, Long logAppendTime) throws IOException {\n                    return DefaultRecord.readPartiallyFrom(inputStream, baseOffset, baseTimestamp, baseSequence, logAppendTime);\n                }\n            };\n        } else {\n            return new StreamRecordIterator(inputStream) {\n                @Override\n                protected Record doReadRecord(long baseOffset, long baseTimestamp, int baseSequence, Long logAppendTime) throws IOException {\n                    return DefaultRecord.readFrom(inputStream, baseOffset, baseTimestamp, baseSequence, logAppendTime);\n                }\n            };\n        }\n    }\n\n    private CloseableIterator<Record> uncompressedIterator() {\n        final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate();\n        buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET);\n        return new RecordIterator() {\n            @Override\n            protected Record readNext(long baseOffset, long baseTimestamp, int baseSequence, Long logAppendTime) {\n                try {\n                    return DefaultRecord.readFrom(buffer, baseOffset, baseTimestamp, baseSequence, logAppendTime);\n                } catch (BufferUnderflowException e) {\n                    throw new InvalidRecordException(\"Incorrect declared batch size, premature EOF reached\");\n                }\n            }\n            @Override\n            protected boolean ensureNoneRemaining() {\n                return !buffer.hasRemaining();\n            }\n            @Override\n            public void close() {}\n        };\n    }\n\n    /**\n     * Returns an iterator over the records in this batch.\n     *\n     * <p>For uncompressed batches, this returns a lazy iterator that reads records on-demand from the buffer.\n     * For compressed batches, the entire batch is decompressed upfront and returned as an in-memory list iterator.\n     * This ensures that decompression resources can be properly released without relying on explicit close() calls.\n     *\n     * <p><b>Memory Characteristics:</b>\n     * <ul>\n     *   <li>Uncompressed: Minimal memory overhead, lazy decoding</li>\n     *   <li>Compressed: Full decompression into memory required; memory usage = O(decompressed batch size)</li>\n     * </ul>\n     *\n     * <p><b>Resource Management:</b> For compressed batches, all decompression happens during this method call.\n     * No explicit resource cleanup is required. For lower memory overhead with large compressed batches, use\n     * {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} instead.\n     *\n     * @return An Iterator over the records in this batch. For empty batches, returns an empty iterator.\n     * @throws InvalidRecordException if the batch is corrupt or has incorrect declared size\n     * @see #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier) for memory-efficient iteration of compressed batches\n     */\n    @Override\n    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {\n        if (count() == 0)\n            return Collections.emptyIterator();\n\n        if (!isCompressed())\n            return uncompressedIterator();\n\n        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,\n        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint",

38. Bash @ -
Input
{
  "command": "head -50 /workspace/documentation.md",
  "description": "Verify documentation file was created"
}
Output
{
  "interrupted": false,
  "isImage": false,
  "noOutputExpected": false,
  "stderr": "",
  "stdout": "# Kafka Record Batch Serialization Javadoc Documentation\n\n## Summary\n\nThis document summarizes the Javadoc enhancements made to Kafka's record batch serialization classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/`. The goal was to provide comprehensive documentation for classes that handle the binary encoding of Kafka message batches, which are critical for correctness and performance.\n\n## Classes Documented\n\n### 1. RecordBatch (Interface)\n**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java`\n\n**Purpose:** Defines the contract for record batches in Kafka protocol, allowing both legacy (magic v0-v1) and modern (magic v2+) batch formats.\n\n#### Enhancements Made:\n- **Class-level Javadoc:** Enhanced to explain the role in Kafka protocol, thread-safety guarantees, and magic versions (0, 1, 2+)\n- **Method Javadoc for `baseOffset()`:** Added performance note clarifying O(n) complexity for magic v0-v1 (requires iteration) and O(1) for magic v2+ (header access)\n- **Method Javadoc for `streamingIterator()`:** Enhanced with detailed performance characteristics explaining lazy decompression and memory efficiency benefits\n\n#### Key Documentation Points:\n- **Thread-Safety:** Stated as implementation-dependent (callers must check specific implementation)\n- **Protocol Role:** Emphasized as fundamental unit of serialization with single header overhead\n- **Magic Versions:** Documented differences between v0 (legacy, no timestamps/producer info), v1 (timestamps), and v2+ (producer ID, sequences, transactional markers)\n- **Performance:** Highlighted that lazy decompression via streaming iterators significantly reduces memory footprint for large batches\n\n### 2. DefaultRecordBatch (Class)\n**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java`\n\n**Purpose:** Concrete implementation of RecordBatch for magic v2+ (current format). Handles serialization/deserialization of modern Kafka record batches with full protocol support.\n\n#### Enhancements Made:\n- **Constructor Javadoc:** Added documentation explaining non-thread-safe nature, buffer ownership, and minimum size requirement\n- **baseTimestamp() method:** Enhanced with explanation of timestamp delta encoding, use in CREATE_TIME vs LOG_APPEND_TIME, and O(1) performance\n- **recordInputStream() method:** Documented the decompression handling and lazy decompression behavior\n- **iterator() method:** Added detailed memory characteristics comparison between compressed (eager decompression) and uncompressed (lazy loading) batches\n- **skipKeyValueIterator() method:** Documented memory efficiency benefits for metadata-only iteration and resource management requirements\n- **streamingIterator() method:** Detailed lazy decompression, thread-safety concerns, and resource cleanup requirements\n- **writeEmptyHeader() static method:** Documented purpose of empty batches for preserving producer state after compaction\n- **writeHeader() static method:** Comprehensive documentation of batch header format, CRC computation, field meanings, and validation requirements\n- **estimateBatchSizeUpperBound() static method:** Clarified that this is an upper bound excluding compression overhead and is useful for buffer pre-allocation\n\n#### Key Documentation Points:\n- **Thread-Safety:** Explicitly stated as not thread-safe; concurrent buffer access causes undefined behavior\n- **Memory Characteristics:** Detailed eager decompression for iterator() (full batch memory) vs lazy for streamingIterator()\n- **Compaction Support:** Explained preservation of base/last offset and sequence for producer state recovery\n- **Batch Format:** Documented the magic v2 schema with all fields and their purposes\n- **CRC Computation:** Clarified that partition leader epoch is excluded from CRC to avoid recomputation at broker\n- **Timestamp Encoding:** Explained delta encoding relative to base timestamp for space efficiency\n\n### 3. MemoryRecords (Class)\n**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java`"
}

      

      

        Code Changes (27)
        
1. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
After
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka protocol. In old versions of the record format
 * (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 *
 * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> Record batches are the fundamental unit of serialization for Kafka message logs.
 * They encapsulate multiple records along with metadata such as offsets, timestamps, compression information, and
 * producer information (for idempotence and transactions). The batch structure allows for efficient storage and
 * transmission of multiple records with a single header overhead.
 *
 * <p><b>Thread-Safety:</b> Implementations may or may not be thread-safe. Callers should consult the implementation
 * documentation for specific thread-safety guarantees. Generally, record batches are immutable after construction
 * (except for {@link MutableRecordBatch}), but this is not universally guaranteed.
 *
 * <p><b>Magic Versions:</b> The magic version indicates the record format:
 * <ul>
 *   <li>Magic 0: Legacy format with no timestamp or producer information</li>
 *   <li>Magic 1: Added timestamp support</li>
 *   <li>Magic 2+: Added producer ID, sequence numbers, transactional markers, and control records</li>
 * </ul>
 *
 * @see {@link MutableRecordBatch} for mutable variants
 * @see {@link MemoryRecords} for in-memory batch collections
 * @see {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for the magic v2+ concrete implementation
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
2. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will
     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will
     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return
     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the
     * behavior is equivalent.
     *
     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with
     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.
     *
     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record
     *         as described above).
     */
    long baseOffset();
After
    /**
     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will
     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will
     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return
     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the
     * behavior is equivalent.
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> This method has O(n) time complexity for magic versions 0 and 1 since it requires
     * iterating through all records to find the first offset. For magic version 2 and above, this is O(1) and accesses
     * the value directly from the batch header. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for
     * all magic versions.
     *
     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record
     *         as described above).
     */
    long baseOffset();
3. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
After
    /**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> This method provides lazy decompression, meaning compressed record data
     * is only decompressed as records are requested. This is more memory-efficient than decompressing the entire batch
     * upfront, especially for large batches. However, it requires the iterator to be properly closed to release
     * decompression resources.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers (e.g., {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier})
     *                                    will have a significant performance impact.
     * @return A closeable iterator over records in this batch. Must be closed to release decompression resources.
     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the batch is corrupt
     * @throws KafkaException if decompression fails
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
4. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    DefaultRecordBatch(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }
After
    /**
     * Creates a record batch from the given buffer containing batch header and records.
     *
     * <p><b>Thread-Safety:</b> This implementation is not thread-safe. Concurrent access to the underlying
     * ByteBuffer by multiple threads will result in undefined behavior. If thread-safety is required, synchronize
     * externally or use immutable copies.
     *
     * @param buffer A ByteBuffer positioned at the start of a magic v2+ record batch. The buffer must contain
     *               a valid batch header of at least {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes.
     */
    DefaultRecordBatch(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }
5. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas.
     *
     * @return The base timestamp
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
After
    /**
     * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas.
     *
     * <p>In the magic v2+ format, individual record timestamps are stored as deltas relative to this base
     * timestamp to save space. For CREATE_TIME timestamp type, this is typically the timestamp of the first
     * record. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, this is the timestamp assigned by the broker. If the batch becomes empty after
     * compaction, this field is set to {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} (-1).
     *
     * <p><b>Performance:</b> This method is O(1) and reads directly from the buffer.
     *
     * @return The base timestamp in milliseconds since Unix epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} if
     *         the batch is empty or the timestamp is undefined.
     * @see #maxTimestamp() for the maximum timestamp in the batch
     * @see TimestampType#CREATE_TIME
     */
    public long baseTimestamp() {
        return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET);
    }
6. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    public InputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate();
        buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET);
        return Compression.of(compressionType()).build().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier);
    }
After
    /**
     * Creates an input stream for reading the records in this batch, handling decompression if necessary.
     *
     * <p>This method handles the decompression setup for compressed batches. For uncompressed batches, it simply
     * wraps the buffer. For compressed batches, it returns a decompression stream.
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> The returned stream does not decompress data until it is actually read.
     * For large compressed batches, this defers memory allocation and CPU cost until records are accessed.
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier Supplies buffers for decompression. For optimal performance with reusable buffers,
     *                       use {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier} or similar.
     * @return An InputStream positioned at the start of the records section, with decompression applied if needed.
     * @throws KafkaException if the compression codec is not available or decompression setup fails
     */
    public InputStream recordInputStream(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        final ByteBuffer buffer = this.buffer.duplicate();
        buffer.position(RECORDS_OFFSET);
        return Compression.of(compressionType()).build().wrapForInput(buffer, magic(), bufferSupplier);
    }
7. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {
        if (count() == 0)
            return Collections.emptyIterator();

        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,
        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint
        // can use `streamingIterator` at the cost of additional complexity
        try (CloseableIterator<Record> iterator = compressedIterator(BufferSupplier.NO_CACHING, false)) {
            List<Record> records = new ArrayList<>(count());
            while (iterator.hasNext())
                records.add(iterator.next());
            return records.iterator();
        }
    }
After
    /**
     * Returns an iterator over the records in this batch.
     *
     * <p>For uncompressed batches, this returns a lazy iterator that reads records on-demand from the buffer.
     * For compressed batches, the entire batch is decompressed upfront and returned as an in-memory list iterator.
     * This ensures that decompression resources can be properly released without relying on explicit close() calls.
     *
     * <p><b>Memory Characteristics:</b>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Uncompressed: Minimal memory overhead, lazy decoding</li>
     *   <li>Compressed: Full decompression into memory required; memory usage = O(decompressed batch size)</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><b>Resource Management:</b> For compressed batches, all decompression happens during this method call.
     * No explicit resource cleanup is required. For lower memory overhead with large compressed batches, use
     * {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} instead.
     *
     * @return An Iterator over the records in this batch. For empty batches, returns an empty iterator.
     * @throws InvalidRecordException if the batch is corrupt or has incorrect declared size
     * @see #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier) for memory-efficient iteration of compressed batches
     */
    @Override
    public Iterator<Record> iterator() {
        if (count() == 0)
            return Collections.emptyIterator();

        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // for a normal iterator, we cannot ensure that the underlying compression stream is closed,
        // so we decompress the full record set here. Use cases which call for a lower memory footprint
        // can use `streamingIterator` at the cost of additional complexity
        try (CloseableIterator<Record>

8. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (count() == 0) {
            return CloseableIterator.wrap(Collections.emptyIterator());
        }

        /*
         * For uncompressed iterator, it is actually not worth skipping key / value / headers at all since
         * its ByteBufferInputStream's skip() function is less efficient compared with just reading it actually
         * as it will allocate new byte array.
         */
        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

        // we define this to be a closable iterator so that caller (i.e. the log validator) needs to close it
        // while we can save memory footprint of not decompressing the full record set ahead of time
        return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, true);
    }
After
    /**
     * Returns a closeable iterator that skips key, value, and header fields for memory efficiency.
     *
     * <p>This method is useful for scenarios where only record metadata (offset, timestamp, etc.) is needed
     * and record payloads can be skipped. For uncompressed batches, actual skipping is not performed since
     * buffer skip operations are less efficient than simply reading the data. For compressed batches, payload
     * skipping is performed during decompression.
     *
     * <p><b>Memory Efficiency:</b> For compressed batches with large payloads, this can significantly reduce
     * memory consumption by avoiding full decompression of record keys and values. For uncompressed batches,
     * the memory savings are negligible since all data must be read anyway.
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier Supplies buffers for decompression in compressed batches
     * @return A closeable iterator over records with payloads skipped. Callers must close this iterator to
     *         release decompression resources, especially important for compressed batches.
     * @see #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier) for full streaming iteration
     * @see #iterator() for standard iteration
     */
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (count() == 0) {
            return CloseableIterator.wrap(Collections.emptyIterator());
        }

        /*
         * For uncompressed iterator, it is actually not worth skipping key / value / headers at all since
         * its ByteBufferInputStream's skip() function is less efficient compared with just reading it actually
         * as it will allocate new byte array.
         */
        if (!isCompressed())
            return uncompressedIterator();

9. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (isCompressed())
            return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false);
        else
            return uncompressedIterator();
    }
After
    /**
     * Returns a closeable iterator with lazy decompression of records.
     *
     * <p>For uncompressed batches, this is equivalent to {@link #iterator()} but returns a closeable iterator.
     * For compressed batches, decompression is deferred until records are actually requested via iteration,
     * reducing memory overhead for batches where not all records are consumed.
     *
     * <p><b>Thread-Safety:</b> The returned iterator is not thread-safe. The underlying decompression state
     * is managed by the iterator, and concurrent access will result in undefined behavior.
     *
     * @param bufferSupplier Supplies buffers for decompression. Reusable suppliers (e.g.,
     *                       {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier}) significantly improve performance
     *                       for repeated iterations or multiple batches.
     * @return A closeable iterator over all records in this batch with lazy decompression. Callers must close
     *         this iterator to release decompression resources.
     * @see #skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier) for memory-efficient metadata-only iteration
     * @see #iterator() for standard iteration that decompresses upfront for compressed batches
     */
    @Override
    public CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) {
        if (isCompressed())
            return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false);
        else
            return uncompressedIterator();
    }
10. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/DefaultRecordBatch.java
Before
    public static void writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                        byte magic,
                                        long producerId,
                                        short producerEpoch,
                                        int baseSequence,
                                        long baseOffset,
                                        long lastOffset,
                                        int partitionLeaderEpoch,
                                        TimestampType timestampType,
                                        long timestamp,
                                        boolean isTransactional,
                                        boolean isControlRecord) {
After
    /**
     * Writes an empty record batch header to the buffer.
     *
     * <p>Empty batches are used to preserve producer state information (base sequence, last sequence) after
     * log compaction, even when all records have been removed. This allows brokers to maintain idempotence
     * and transaction state correctly.
     *
     * <p><b>Performance:</b> This method writes {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes to the buffer.
     * The CRC is computed over the batch header and record data (none for empty batches).
     *
     * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write to. Must have at least {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes remaining.
     * @param magic The magic version (must be {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE})
     * @param producerId The producer ID for idempotent/transactional producers, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID}
     * @param producerEpoch The producer epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH}

     * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME)
     * @param timestamp The timestamp for this batch (max timestamp or delete horizon)
     * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction

     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic version is invalid or timestamp is invalid

    /**
     * Writes a record batch header to the buffer with the specified parameters.
     *
     * <p>This method writes the complete batch header (including all fields) and computes the CRC-32C checksum
     * over the batch. After writing, the buffer position is advanced to the end of the header, ready for writing
     * record data.
     *
     * <p><b>Batch Header Format (for magic v2):</b>
     * <pre>
     * | BaseOffset (8) | Length (4) | PartitionLeaderEpoch (4) | Magic (1) |
     * | CRC (4) | Attributes (2) | LastOffsetDelta (4) | BaseTimestamp (8) |
     * | MaxTimestamp (8) | ProducerId (8) | ProducerEpoch (2) | BaseSequence (4) |
     * | RecordsCount (4) | [Records] |
     * </pre>
     *
     * <p><b>CRC Computation:</b> The CRC covers from the Attributes field through the end of the batch
     * (including record data). The PartitionLeaderEpoch is explicitly excluded to avoid recomputation
     * when the epoch is updated after receipt by a broker.
     *
     * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write to. Position should be at the start of the header.
     *               Must have at least sizeInBytes bytes remaining.
     * @param baseOffset The offset of the first record in the batch
     * @param lastOffsetDelta The offset delta of the last record (lastOffset - baseOffset)
     * @param sizeInBytes Total batch size including the base offset and length fields ({@code RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD + records size})
     * @param magic The magic version (must be {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE})
     * @param compressionType The compression algorithm used for records (NONE, GZIP, SNAPPY, LZ4, ZSTD)
     * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME)
     * @param baseTimestamp Base timestamp for record timestamp deltas, or {@
... (truncated)

Before
    /**
     * Get an upper bound on the size of a batch with only a single record using a given key and value. This
     * is only an estimate because it does not take into account additional overhead from the compression
     * algorithm used.
     */
    static int estimateBatchSizeUpperBound(ByteBuffer key, ByteBuffer value, Header[] headers) {
        return RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD + DefaultRecord.recordSizeUpperBound(key, value, headers);
    }
After
    /**
     * Get an upper bound on the size of a batch with only a single record using a given key and value.
     *
     * <p>This is only an estimate and does not take into account:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Additional overhead from the compression algorithm used (e.g., frame headers for LZ4)</li>
     *   <li>Memory padding or alignment</li>
     *   <li>Stream format overhead for compressed batches</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><b>Use Case:</b> Useful for buffer pre-allocation to avoid reallocation during batch building.
     *
     * @param key The record key, or null for null keys
     * @param value The record value, or null for null values
     * @param headers The record headers, or empty array for no headers
     * @return An upper bound on the serialized size in bytes. Actual size may be smaller due to compression.
     * @see DefaultRecord#recordSizeUpperBound(ByteBuffer, ByteBuffer, Header[])
     */
    static int estimateBatchSizeUpperBound(ByteBuffer key, ByteBuffer value, Header[] headers) {
        return RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD + DefaultRecord.recordSizeUpperBound(key, value, headers);
    }

/**
 * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used only for reading or
 * modifying in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer see {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder},
 * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)} variants.
 */
public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {
After
/**
 * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used for reading, writing, and modifying
 * in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder}
 * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)} variants.
 *
 * <p><b>Role in Kafka Protocol:</b> MemoryRecords is the primary in-memory representation of serialized Kafka
 * record batches. It provides methods for iterating over batches, filtering records by retention policies,
 * and serializing to various output formats (ByteBuffer, channel, output stream).
 *
 * <p><b>Thread-Safety:</b> This class is not thread-safe. The underlying ByteBuffer is shared with callers, and
 * concurrent modification (e.g., changing the buffer position or limit) will cause undefined behavior. Use external
 * synchronization or create separate instances for concurrent access.
 *
 * <p><b>Memory Efficiency:</b> MemoryRecords provides lazy iteration over batches and lazy decompression of
 * records via {@link RecordBatch#streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}. This allows for processing large record
 * sets with bounded memory usage.
 *
 * <p><b>Filtering and Compaction:</b> The {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} method
 * supports in-place record filtering for log compaction and tombstone deletion. This method handles batch
 * rebuilding with updated metadata as needed.
 *
 * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for building new record buffers
 * @see RecordBatch for individual batch interface
 * @see DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ concrete implementation
 */
public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {
14. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    // Construct a writable memory records
    private MemoryRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        Objects.requireNonNull(buffer, "buffer should not be null");
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }
After
    /**
     * Creates a MemoryRecords instance wrapping the given buffer.
     *
     * <p><b>Thread-Safety:</b> This class is not thread-safe. Concurrent access to the buffer (including position
     * and limit modifications) will result in undefined behavior. The caller retains ownership of the buffer.
     *
     * <p><b>Buffer Semantics:</b> The buffer should be positioned at the start of the record data and contain
     * complete {@link RecordBatch} structures. The limit determines the end of valid record data.
     *
     * @param buffer The ByteBuffer containing serialized record batches. Must not be null.
     * @throws NullPointerException if buffer is null
     */
    private MemoryRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        Objects.requireNonNull(buffer, "buffer should not be null");
        this.buffer = buffer;
    }
15. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * The total number of bytes in this message set not including any partial, trailing messages. This
     * may be smaller than what is returned by {@link #sizeInBytes()}.
     * @return The number of valid bytes
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
After
    /**
     * The total number of bytes in this message set not including any partial, trailing messages.
     * This may be smaller than what is returned by {@link #sizeInBytes()}.
     *
     * <p>Valid bytes are computed by iterating through all batches and summing their sizes. Trailing partial
     * batches (incomplete due to network truncation or buffer size limits) are excluded from this count.
     * This is useful for determining how much of the buffer contains complete, valid batch data.
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> This method caches the result after the first call. If the underlying buffer
     * is modified, the cached value is not invalidated; callers must ensure they do not modify the buffer
     * after calling this method or create a new MemoryRecords instance.
     *
     * @return The number of bytes containing complete, valid batches. May be less than {@link #sizeInBytes()}
     *         if partial batches are present.
     * @see #sizeInBytes() for the total buffer size including partial batches
     */
    public int validBytes() {
        if (validBytes >= 0)
            return validBytes;

        int bytes = 0;
        for (RecordBatch batch : batches())
            bytes += batch.sizeInBytes();

        this.validBytes = bytes;
        return bytes;
    }
16. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
After
    /**
     * Filters records according to a {@link RecordFilter} and writes the filtered result to a ByteBuffer.
     *
     * <p><b>Filtering Process:</b> This method iterates through all batches, applying batch-level retention logic,
     * then record-level retention logic. Batches and records that fail the filter are dropped. If a batch is partially
     * filtered (some records retained, some dropped), the batch is rebuilt with updated metadata. This includes:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Recompressing the filtered records if the original batch was compressed</li>
     *   <li>Updating delete horizon timestamps if tombstones are present</li>
     *   <li>Preserving base and last offset information for producer state recovery</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Characteristics:</b> Memory usage depends on buffer supplier caching:
     * <ul>
     *   <li>With {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier}: Decompression buffers are reused across batches</li>
     *   <li>With {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING}: New buffers allocated for each compressed batch (higher GC pressure)</li>
     * </ul>
     * For large compressed batches, using a reusable supplier is strongly recommended.
     *
     * <p><b>Output Buffer Management:</b> If the filtered records do not fit in the provided destinationBuffer,
     * a new buffer is allocated and returned via {@link FilterResult#outputBuffer()}. Callers should check for
     * buffer overflow and handle it appropriately (e.g., re-attempt with larger buffer or chain filters).
     *
     * @param filter The filter logic to apply. Implementers should override
     *               {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} and
     *               {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)}.
... (truncated)
17. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    @Override
    public MemoryRecords slice(int position, int size) {
        if (position < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid position: " + position + " in read from " + this);
        if (position > buffer.limit())
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Slice from position " + position + " exceeds end position of " + this);
        if (size < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid size: " + size + " in read from " + this);

        int availableBytes = Math.min(size, buffer.limit() - position);
        // As of now, clients module support Java11 hence can't use ByteBuffer::slice(position, size) method.
        // So we need to create a duplicate buffer and set the position and limit. Duplicate buffer
        // is backed by original bytes hence not the content but only the relative position and limit
        // are changed in the duplicate buffer. Once the position and limit are set, we can call the
        // slice method to get the sliced buffer, which is a backed by the original buffer with the
        // position reset to 0 and limit set to the size of the slice.
        ByteBuffer slicedBuffer = buffer.duplicate();
        slicedBuffer.position(position);
        slicedBuffer.limit(position + availableBytes);
        // Reset the position to 0 so that the sliced view has a relative position.
        slicedBuffer = slicedBuffer.slice();

        return readableRecords(slicedBuffer);
    }
After
    /**
     * Creates a new MemoryRecords instance representing a slice of this buffer.
     *
     * <p><b>Memory Efficiency:</b> The returned MemoryRecords shares the underlying byte array with the original
     * buffer but provides a view starting at the specified position. This is a zero-copy operation that does not
     * allocate new memory for the record data itself (though a new ByteBuffer view is created).
     *
     * <p><b>Position and Limit:</b> The returned MemoryRecords will iterate over records starting at the given
     * position. If the actual available bytes are less than the requested size, the available bytes are used.
     * The slice must represent complete record batches; partial batches at the boundaries may be skipped
     * by the batch iterator.
     *
     * @param position The byte offset in this buffer to start the slice (0-based, relative to buffer start)
     * @param size The maximum number of bytes to include in the slice. Actual slice size may be smaller
     *             if fewer bytes are available.
     * @return A new MemoryRecords instance representing the sliced region. Modifications to the original
     *         buffer are visible through the slice (and vice versa).
     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if position is negative, exceeds buffer limit, or size is negative
     * @see #buffer() for accessing the underlying ByteBuffer
     */
    @Override
    public MemoryRecords slice(int position, int size) {
        if (position < 0)
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid position: " + position + " in read from " + this);
        if (position > buffer.limit())
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Slice from position " + position + " exceeds end position of " + this);
        if (size < 0)
     
... (truncated)

    public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        return new MemoryRecords(buffer);

After
    /**
     * Creates a MemoryRecords instance for reading from the given buffer.
     *
     * <p>This is the primary factory method for creating readable MemoryRecords instances. The buffer should
     * contain serialized {@link RecordBatch} structures. To create writable (mutable) MemoryRecords with new
     * batches, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} instead.
     *
     * <p><b>Thread-Safety:</b> The returned instance is not thread-safe. Concurrent access to the underlying
     * buffer will cause undefined behavior.
     *
     * @param buffer A ByteBuffer containing serialized record batches. The buffer position and limit determine
     *               the readable region. The position should be at the start of valid record data.
     * @return A MemoryRecords instance wrapping the buffer
     * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches
     * @see #buffer() for accessing the underlying ByteBuffer from a MemoryRecords instance
     */
    public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) {
        return new MemoryRecords(buffer);
    }
19. EDIT 
Before
    /**
     * Get the byte buffer that backs this instance for reading.
     */
    public ByteBuffer buffer() {
        return buffer.duplicate();
    }
After
    /**
     * Get the byte buffer that backs this instance for reading.
     *
     * <p>Returns a duplicate of the underlying buffer, so position and limit changes to the returned buffer
     * do not affect the internal state of this MemoryRecords instance. However, the buffer shares the same
     * backing byte array, so modifications to the buffer's content are visible.
     *
     * <p><b>Use Cases:</b>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Serializing records to a channel or output stream</li>
     *   <li>Passing record data to other layers (e.g., network transport)</li>
     *   <li>Direct memory operations requiring ByteBuffer manipulation</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * @return A duplicate of the underlying buffer. The position is at the start of the record data.
     *         The limit is at the end of the record data.
     * @see #slice(int, int) for creating a view of a portion of the records
     */
    public ByteBuffer buffer() {
        return buffer.duplicate();
    }

21. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Validates the header of the first batch and returns batch size.
     * @return first batch size including LOG_OVERHEAD if buffer contains header up to
     *         magic byte, null otherwise
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if record size or magic is invalid
     */
    public Integer firstBatchSize() {
        if (buffer.remaining() < HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC)
            return null;
        return new ByteBufferLogInputStream(buffer, Integer.MAX_VALUE).nextBatchSize();
    }
After
    /**
     * Validates the header of the first batch and returns its size.
     *
     * <p>This method checks if the buffer contains sufficient data for reading a complete batch header
     * (up to and including the magic byte and batch size field). It then parses and returns the size
     * of the first batch. This is useful for incremental batch reading and validation.
     *
     * <p><b>Partial Batch Handling:</b> If the buffer contains less than {@link AbstractRecords#HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC}
     * bytes, null is returned to indicate that the batch header is incomplete. The caller can wait for more data
     * before retrying.
     *
     * @return The size of the first batch including {@link AbstractRecords#LOG_OVERHEAD} bytes if the buffer
     *         contains a complete header, or null if the header is incomplete or the buffer is empty.
     * @throws CorruptRecordException if the batch size field is invalid (e.g., negative or exceeds reasonable limits)
     *                                  or if the magic byte is invalid
     * @see AbstractRecords#LOG_OVERHEAD for the size overhead
     * @see AbstractRecords#HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC for the minimum header size needed
     */
    public Integer firstBatchSize() {
        if (buffer.remaining() < HEADER_SIZE_UP_TO_MAGIC)
            return null;
        return new ByteBufferLogInputStream(buffer, Integer.MAX_VALUE).nextBatchSize();
    }

    /**
     * Creates a builder for writing record batches to the given buffer with default parameters.
     *
     * <p><b>Defaults:</b>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)</li>
     *   <li>Log append time: Current system time if timestampType is LOG_APPEND_TIME, otherwise NO_TIMESTAMP</li>
     *   <li>Producer ID: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} (no idempotence/transactions)</li>
     *   <li>Transactional: false</li>
     *   <li>Control batch: false</li>
     *   <li>Partition leader epoch: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH}</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><b>Use Case:</b> Simple producers that don't require idempotence or transactions.
     *
     * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write batches to. Must have sufficient capacity.
     * @param compression The compression algorithm to use for records in batches
     * @param timestampType CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME. If LOG_APPEND_TIME, the current system time is used.
     * @param baseOffset The offset of the first record in the first batch
     * @return A MemoryRecordsBuilder for appending records
     * @see #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long, long) for more control over parameters
     * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for the full builder interface
     */
    public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                               Compression compression,
                                               TimestampType timestampType,
                                               long baseOffset) {
        return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset);
    }

    /**
     * Creates a MemoryRecords instance containing the given records in a single batch.
     *
     * <p><b>Batch Parameters:</b>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)</li>
     *   <li>Base offset: 0</li>
     *   <li>Timestamp type: CREATE_TIME</li>
     *   <li>Producer ID: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID}</li>
     *   <li>Transactional: false</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><b>Use Case:</b> Convenient factory for creating test data or simple record sets. For production use,
     * consider using {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} for more control over batch parameters.
     *
     * @param compression The compression algorithm to apply to records in the batch
     * @param records The records to include. Empty records array creates an empty MemoryRecords instance.
     * @return A MemoryRecords instance containing the records in a single batch
     * @see #withIdempotentRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for idempotent producers
     * @see #withTransactionalRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for transactional producers
     */
    public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord... records) {
        return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, records);
    }

    public static MemoryRecords withTransactionalRecords(Compression compression, long producerId,

After
    /**
     * Creates a MemoryRecords instance with records from a transactional producer.
     *
     * <p>Transactional batches are part of multi-partition transactions managed by the broker. They contain
     * application records that may be rolled back or committed as an atomic unit. This method creates batches
     * with the transactional flag set.
     *
     * <p><b>Batch Parameters:</b>
     * <ul>
     *   <li>Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)</li>
     *   <li>Base offset: 0</li>
     *   <li>Timestamp type: CREATE_TIME</li>
     *   <li>Partition leader epoch: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH}</li>
     *   <li>Transactional: true</li>
     * </ul>
     *
     * <p><b>Transaction Markers:</b> In addition to application records, transactions also include control records
     * (commit/abort markers) that are added separately via {@link #withEndTransactionMarker(long, long, short, EndTransactionMarker)}.
     *
     * @param compression The compression algorithm for records
     * @param producerId The ID of the producer (assigned by broker)
     * @param producerEpoch The epoch of the producer
     * @param baseSequence The base sequence number for the first record
     * @param records The records to include in this transaction batch
     * @return A MemoryRecords instance with transactional metadata
     * @see #withEndTransactionMarker(long, long, short, EndTransactionMarker) for transaction markers
     * @see #withIdempotentRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for idempotent non-transactional
     */
    public static MemoryRecords withTransactionalRecords(Compression compression, long producerId,
                                                         short producerEpoch, int baseSequence, Simple
... (truncated)
26. EDIT /workspace/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/MemoryRecords.java
Before
    /**
     * Write all records to the given channel (including partial records).
     * @param channel The channel to write to
     * @return The number of bytes written
     * @throws IOException For any IO errors writing to the channel
     */
    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {
        buffer.mark();
        int written = 0;
        while (written < sizeInBytes())
            written += channel.write(buffer);
        buffer.reset();
        return written;
    }
After
    /**
     * Writes all records to the given channel, including partial records.
     *
     * <p>This method repeatedly calls {@link GatheringByteChannel#write(ByteBuffer)} until all bytes
     * are written. It saves and restores the buffer position, so this instance's iteration state is
     * not affected by the write.
     *
     * <p><b>Partial Records:</b> Unlike {@link #validBytes()}, this method writes the entire buffer
     * including any partial/incomplete records at the end. This is useful when the exact buffer size
     * must be transmitted, such as when the buffer is part of a larger protocol frame.
     *
     * <p><b>Performance Note:</b> For efficient network transmission, this method uses a single
     * GatheringByteChannel, which can write from multiple buffers in a single system call.
     * However, multiple invocations may be needed if the channel write buffer fills.
     *
     * @param channel A GatheringByteChannel to write to (e.g., SocketChannel, FileChannel)
     * @return The total number of bytes written. Equals {@link #sizeInBytes()}.
     * @throws IOException If an I/O error occurs writing to the channel
     * @see #validBytes() for writing only complete batches
     * @see #writeTo(TransferableChannel, int, int) for writing to TransferableChannel
     */
    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {
        buffer.mark();
        int written = 0;
        while (written < sizeInBytes())
            written += channel.write(buffer);
        buffer.reset();
        return written;
    }
27. WRITE /workspace/documentation.md
# Kafka Record Batch Serialization Javadoc Documentation

## Summary

This document summarizes the Javadoc enhancements made to Kafka's record batch serialization classes in `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/`. The goal was to provide comprehensive documentation for classes that handle the binary encoding of Kafka message batches, which are critical for correctness and performance.

## Classes Documented

### 1. RecordBatch (Interface)
**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/common/record/internal/RecordBatch.java`

**Purpose:** Defines the contract for record batches in Kafka protocol, allowing both legacy (magic v0-v1) and modern (magic v2+) batch formats.

#### Enhancements Made:
- **Class-level Javadoc:** Enhanced to explain the role in Kafka protocol, thread-safety guarantees, and magic versions (0, 1, 2+)
- **Method Javadoc for `baseOffset()`:** Added performance note clarifying O(n) complexity for magic v0-v1 (requires iteration) and O(1) for magic v2+ (header access)
- **Method Javadoc for `streamingIterator()`:** Enhanced with detailed performance characteristics explaining lazy decompression and memory efficiency benefits

#### Key Documentation Points:
- **Thread-Safety:** Stated as implementation-dependent (callers must check specific implementation)
- **Protocol Role:** Emphasized as fundamental unit of serialization with single header overhead
- **Magic Versions:** Documented differences between v0 (legacy, no timestamps/producer info), v1 (timestamps), and v2+ (producer ID, sequences, transactional markers)
- **Performance:** Highlighted that lazy decompression via streaming iterators significantly reduces memory footprint for large batches

### 2. DefaultRecordBatch (Class)
**Location:** `clients/src/main/java/org/apache
